summary: Drew DeVault defends freedesktop.org's decision to ban Hyprland maintainer Vaxry from FDO community spaces, providing detailed context on the events leading to the ban. He argues that FDO's conduct enforcement team acted with restraint and within their mandate, while Vaxry escalated the situation by construing a polite warning as threats, minimizing his own hate speech, and threatening legal action. DeVault recounts his own failed private efforts over 18 months to help Vaxry change his behavior, culminating in Vaxry expressing openness to arguments for genocide. He calls on the broader Linux community to protect itself from the toxic culture Hyprland has fostered and condemns the harassment directed at the FDO officer involved.
key_points: FDO's conduct team issued a restrained private warning based on credible complaints, and Vaxry's hostile response to that warning itself justified the eventual ban
Vaxry's public appeals frame himself as the victim while employing dog-whistles that directed harassment toward the FDO officer who contacted him
DeVault spent months in private discussions trying to help Vaxry reform, efforts that ended when Vaxry expressed openness to arguments for genocide
The Hyprland community's ban on 'political' speech in practice silences LGBTQ discussion while permitting hate speech disguised as humor
The response from communities like /r/linux — slurs, death threats, and harassment of the FDO officer — validates the characterization of Hyprland's community as deeply toxic
money_quotes: The fact of the matter is that Hyprland remains a toxic community, enabled and encouraged by its toxic leadership, namely Vaxry.
Vaxry construes the FDO officer's polite and well-reasoned warning as threats and intimidation. He minimizes examples of his own hate speech by shrugging them off as a joke.
Every option other than banning Vaxry has been exhausted over the past year and a half.
The last thing he said to me was 'I do believe there could be arguments to sway my opinion towards genocide'.
There's nothing left to do but to build a fence around Hyprland and protect the rest of the community from them.
You are trapped in an echo chamber of hate, anger, and bigotry. I hope that you find a way out, and that someday you can build a community which is as great as your software is.
In the end, the Hyprland community's fundamental problem is that they're all 'just having fun', and it seems that they can't have 'fun' unless it's at someone else's expense.
key_insight: When a project leader refuses every opportunity to reform toxic community norms, the broader open source ecosystem has both the right and obligation to isolate that community to protect everyone else.
